string.text: Heads up: %player_name% is operating in the Zone. Known for leaving bodies behind. Eyes open.
%player_name% has a reputation for violence even by Zone standards. Don't mistake them for a neutral.
Heard they nearly wiped a whole squad last month over a disagreement about loot. Nobody walked away clean.
Point is — don't give %player_name% a reason. They don't need much of one.
Word out of Rostok: %player_name% is someone you don't want to be in debt to.
The name %player_name% keeps coming up in connection with things I'd rather not repeat on an open channel.
You know Yevgeny? Tall guy, scar on his chin? He owed %player_name% something. Nobody's seen him in weeks.
I'm not saying anything. I'm just saying keep your debts paid and your mouth shut.
%player_name%'s been burning bridges across the Zone. Whatever their plan is, it's not gentle.
Just stay out of their way. No loot is worth a firefight with that one.
Heard they walked out of Limansk without a scratch while everyone they came in with didn't make it. Convenient.
Could've been circumstance. Could've been intention. Either way, not someone I'm turning my back on.
%player_name% rolled through the Garbage checkpoint yesterday. The guards let them pass without a word. Didn't even ask for ID.
Smart guards. The ones who pushed back on %player_name% before aren't posting duty anymore.
There's fear and then there's whatever %player_name% has built out here. It's different.
Yeah. Fear makes people careful. That kind of reputation makes people disappear before there's ever a confrontation.
You hear about the mercs who tried to squeeze %player_name% for a cut near the Warehouses?
I heard some version of it. Didn't end well for the mercs, I assume.
Three of them walked up. One walked away. He wouldn't say what happened to the other two.
And %player_name%?
Kept walking. Didn't even break stride, from what he said.
I've been in the Zone long enough to know trouble by how people walk. %player_name% walks like trouble.
What do you mean?
Head always moving. Never stops at a bottleneck. Never has their back to a door. Everything's been practiced a hundred times.
That's not paranoia, that's someone who's already been in more bad situations than they care to count.
Exactly. And survived every single one.
%player_name% has been making the rounds. Hard to read, but consistent. Seems to get the job done.
Anyone dealt with %player_name%? Name keeps coming up on the contracts board.
I worked adjacent to them once. Didn't talk much, but they pulled their weight. No complaints from me.
That's more than I can say for half the people coming through here. I'll keep an eye on them.
%player_name% passed through our checkpoint yesterday. Clean. Professional. Didn't make trouble.
Can't get a read on %player_name%. Some say one thing, some say another. Watch, don't judge yet.
Neutral's fine by me. A stalker who doesn't make enemies also doesn't create problems. I'll take it.
Sure. Or they're waiting to show their hand. Either way, eyes open.
Heard %player_name% turned down a hit contract on a Loner camp. Said it wasn't their kind of work.
Interesting. Either they've got principles or they calculated the risk wasn't worth the payout.
Could be both. I've seen plenty of people in this Zone who are principled and still very dangerous.
Fair. Worth noting either way. Someone who picks their fights is harder to predict than someone who takes everything.
%player_name% bought a round at the bar last night. Didn't say much. Just sat and listened.
People who buy drinks and say nothing are either very relaxed or very focused. Hard to tell which with that one.
Barkeep said they left a decent tip and asked about patrol timing near the Swamp. So, probably focused.
Gathering intel over vodka. Could be worse methods. At least they're paying for it.
%player_name%'s been taking small jobs. Nothing flashy. Courier runs, zone mapping, basic escort.
Building a record or staying under the radar?
Could be either. Could be building trust with people before they need something bigger. Smart long game if so.
Or they're just cautious. Some stalkers take time before they commit to anything. Nothing wrong with that.
True. Patience is underrated out here. Zone doesn't reward the reckless.
%player_name%'s name carries real weight now. Heard veterans go quiet when it came up at the bar.
If %player_name% offers you backup, take it. I've seen what they can do and we're lucky they're on our side.
Side's a strong word. %player_name% helps who they want to help. But when they're helping you, there's no one better to have.
Fair enough. I'll take that over nothing.
%player_name% pulled a squad out of a controller nest near Yantar. No casualties. Just walked out.
The Zone's been trying to kill %player_name% since they got here. It keeps failing. That means something.
I talked to one of the guys they pulled out. Said %player_name% didn't seem afraid. Just... methodical. Like it was a problem to solve.
That's what separates good stalkers from great ones. The panic gets quiet and the mind stays clear.
Hope I get there someday. Right now the panic is very loud.
Show %player_name% respect if you see them. That stalker has earned it a hundred times over.
%player_name% cracked a contract last week that three other groups walked away from. Alive and intact.
I heard the payout was enough to fund a full outpost for a season. Not that %player_name% spent it on that.
What did they spend it on?
Better gear. Better mods. Right back into the Zone within a week. Doesn't stop, that one.
Ask me who I'd want beside me in a bad situation and the answer is %player_name%. No hesitation.
You've worked with them?
Once. Short job near the Cordon. Quick and clean. They communicated well, covered their angles, and didn't do anything stupid. Rare combination.
That's high praise from you. You're usually complaining about everybody.
Because most people deserve it. %player_name% is the exception.
People around the campfire go quiet when %player_name%'s name comes up. Not scared quiet. Respectful quiet. There's a difference.
I know the one. Like when someone brings up an old war and the veterans stop talking.
Exactly. The stories are out there, but the people who were actually there don't tend to tell them lightly.
Because the real ones never sound as good as they should. Too much got lost along the way.
Ran into %player_name% at Skadovsk. They bought a round, didn't make a scene. Just another stalker at the bar.
That's how the real ones carry it. No posturing. No speeches. Just show up, do the work, drink, sleep, repeat.
Somebody at the table started asking about the Yantar job. %player_name% deflected every question. Said it was nothing special.
Nothing special. They walked six stalkers out of a controller nest. Nothing special.
That's what I'm saying. That's the whole thing right there.
%player_name% is still finding their footing out here. Rookies who listen tend to survive longer. Hope they're listening.
New face, %player_name%. Hasn't done anything stupid yet. Out here, that's already an achievement.
I told them to keep their detector on and their mouth shut around the veterans. Good advice for anyone starting out.
Did they take it?
Seemed to. Time will tell. The Zone has a way of sorting out who was actually listening.
%player_name% is green but they've got the right instincts. Zone will sort the rest out.
Give them time. Everyone starts as a rookie.
True. The ones who don't make it usually think they know more than they do in the first week.
%player_name% asked good questions when I met them. The kind of questions a person asks when they know they don't know enough. That's promising.
Saw %player_name% near the Cordon this morning. Still has that look — eyes too wide, checking everything twice.
Better too wide than half-closed. Some rookies walk around like they're on a stroll and end up in an anomaly.
Fair. I'll admit the alertness is reassuring. Shows they take it seriously.
Just needs to learn when to relax a little. Staying tense burns you out fast. Precision over constant dread.
That'll come with time. Or it won't, and they'll be gone before it matters.
%player_name% nearly stumbled into a Whirligig near the old factory. Stopped just in time.
Instinct or luck?
Detector beeped. They listened. So a bit of both, honestly.
The ones who listen to their detectors tend to stick around. Good sign.
%player_name%'s still figuring out what to carry and what to leave behind. Saw them loaded down like a pack mule near the Bar.
Every new stalker does that. Pack everything, regret it on the first long run.
They'll learn. Usually takes one bad situation where you need to move fast and can't.
Yeah. The Zone teaches light and fast better than any veteran could.
%player_name% knows how to move in the Zone now. You can tell — no wasted steps, no panic.
Spent time at a camp with %player_name%. Solid. Respects the Zone, respects the people in it.
That respect thing is what separates the competent from the dead. You can be skilled and still get killed by arrogance.
%player_name% doesn't seem the arrogant type. Quiet confidence. Knows what they know, doesn't pretend beyond it.
That's a good place to be at this stage. Lots of room left to grow without the ego getting in the way.
%player_name% has been running good work. Reliable. That's rarer than it sounds out here.
Agreed. Good to have dependable stalkers around.
They finished that escort job last week after two other groups pulled out. Didn't make a big deal of it, just did it.
That's the thing. The reliable ones never make noise about being reliable. It's just how they operate.
%player_name% came through an anomaly cluster near Yantar that I wouldn't attempt on a bet. Clean exit, no fuss.
Yantar anomaly fields are brutal. Either you know what you're doing or you don't come back.
They mapped a route on the way through, apparently. Posted it for anyone who needs it. Didn't charge for it.
That's the kind of thing that builds real goodwill. People remember that.
Saw %player_name% patch up a wounded stalker near the Swamp. Knew what they were doing — proper field dressing, checked for infection.
Field medicine is one of those skills that gets you taken seriously fast. Anyone can shoot straight. Not everyone can keep someone alive afterward.
The stalker made it, from what I heard. Walked out with %player_name% a few hours later.
Solid. %player_name%'s building the right kind of reputation — the kind that means people want to work alongside you.
%player_name% spotted a bandit ambush near the Garbage before it triggered. Warned the group and they skirted around it.
Good situational awareness. That's experience paying off.
The green ones just walk into it. The experienced ones notice something's off before they can even say what.
%player_name%'s reached that point. Their gut catches things before their brain processes them.
That's when the Zone starts feeling less like an enemy and more like... something you can read.
%player_name% has been out here long enough to know what the Zone can do. And survived it anyway.
When %player_name% gives advice about the Zone, I listen. That's years of hard experience talking.
They told me once — every mistake the Zone lets you survive is a lesson. The ones it doesn't let you survive, someone else has to learn from your body.
Grim way to put it. But accurate.
That's how they think. Unsentimental. Useful.
%player_name% doesn't get rattled anymore. Seen things that would break others and just kept going.
True. I'd trust them at my back any day.
There's a certain kind of stillness in the good veterans. They've processed enough that nothing surprises them the wrong way anymore.
%player_name% has that. Quiet under fire. Makes decisions when others freeze.
The Zone breaks most people or makes them. Not many come out the other side like that.
New stalkers at the camp were asking about %player_name%. Wanted to know who taught them.
What did you tell them?
The truth. The Zone taught them. Same as everyone who lasts long enough. There's no shortcut and no school for it.
Bet that wasn't the answer they were hoping for.
It never is. But it's the only honest one.
%player_name% helped coordinate the defense at Yanov last month when things got chaotic. Kept people from running into each other's crossfire.
That kind of situational command is hard to teach. You either see the whole picture or you don't.
%player_name% sees it. I watched them redirect three different groups in the space of two minutes without raising their voice.
Calm commands in a firefight. That's what veterans look like from the outside. Inside, I'm told, it's still terrifying. They just don't show it.
I asked %player_name% how many times they'd been close to dying in the Zone. They laughed.
What's funny about that?
They said they stopped counting around the fortieth time. Said after a while you stop thinking of it as close calls and start thinking of it as Tuesday.
That's either the healthiest or the most concerning thing I've ever heard from a stalker.
Both, probably. They're still here, so I'll take it.
%player_name% is what the Zone makes of people who refuse to die. A legend, whether they want to be or not.
I heard the name %player_name% before I ever set foot in the Zone. Now I understand why.
What was your impression when you finally met them?
Quiet. Ordinary looking. Which made the whole thing more unsettling, not less.
Legends never look like the stories. That's how they become legends. The ones who look dangerous tend to get killed faster.
Not many reach %player_name%'s level. The ones who do don't talk about how they did it. Neither do they.
Let their actions speak. They always do.
I tried to get the story out of someone who rode with them early on. They just shook their head and said some things don't translate.
That's fair. The Zone at that depth isn't something you can explain to someone who hasn't been there.
And %player_name% has been there. Deeper and more often than almost anyone still breathing.
Younger stalkers look at %player_name% like they're looking at a ghost story that turned out to be true.
Because that's basically what they are. Stories first, person second.
The gap between reputation and reality must be strange to live with.
Maybe that's why they keep moving. Hard to be a myth when you're still making runs and sleeping in a camp like everyone else.
Or maybe they just don't think about it. People at that level usually don't have the luxury of philosophy.
They said %player_name% once navigated the Red Forest blind — detector damaged, in fog, after a blowout. Made it out without a scratch.
I don't believe that.
Neither did I. Then I talked to the person who found them on the other side. They weren't joking.
How do you navigate the Red Forest without sensors?
I asked the same thing. Apparently %player_name% said the Zone has patterns, once you've been through enough times you feel the wrong spots before you see them.
That's either wisdom or something the Zone did to them. Hard to tell the difference at that level.
The thing about %player_name% is they still take the small jobs. Nobody at their level has to, but they do.
Why would they?
I think they remember what it was like to need someone who didn't have to help, and helping anyway.
Or they just like being in the Zone. Some people are done the minute they get enough to leave. Others never really want to leave.
%player_name%'s been here long enough to have left a dozen times over. I think you might be right about that second part.
I've met three people in the Zone I would genuinely call irreplaceable. %player_name% is one of them.
Who are the other two?
Dead. Both of them. Which is why I'm careful about that word.
And %player_name% keeps not being dead.
So far. The Zone hasn't stopped trying. But so far.
%player_name% just registered on the general Free Stalker network. Welcome to the brotherhood, such as it is.
Good to see another stalker who thinks for themselves. No commanders, no politics.
Just don't expect a rescue squad if you wander into a bloodsucker nest. Watch your own back out there.
Looks like %player_name% decided to walk the Zone as a Free Stalker. Good choice.
No orders to follow, but no safety net either. The true stalker way.
Amen. Keep your PDA on, %player_name%. We look out for our own when we can.
Word from the Rookie Village: %player_name% is officially flying the Free Stalker colors.
Another soul looking to make their fortune in the Zone without kissing a general's boots.
The Zone provides, but it takes its toll. Trade fair, shoot straight, and don't steal from stashes.
I'll drink to that. There's always room at the campfire, %player_name%.
PDA net just pinged. %player_name% is officially registered as a Free Stalker. Welcome to the grind.
Hope your anomaly detector has fresh batteries. The good artifacts don't find themselves.
And don't let Sidorovich or Barkeep fleece you on your first haul. Know the market prices.
Good luck out there, %player_name%. May the Zone be kind to you.
Looks like %player_name% decided not to play soldier for Duty or get brainwashed by a faction. Welcome to the independent network.
Being a Free Stalker means you answer to nobody but the Zone itself. It's the only real way to live out here.
Damn straight. Just remember: if you hear an SOS on this channel and you're nearby, you'd better answer it.
We might walk our own paths, but we survive together. See you on the trail, %player_name%.
%player_name% is one of us now. Welcome to the free side of the Zone, brother.
We don't do initiation ceremonies. Someone pour %player_name% a drink and call it done.
I've got a fresh bottle of Cossacks waiting. First round is on the rookie!
Fresh blood in Freedom. %player_name% — heard the name before. Good to have them.
One more against the fascists. The Zone's a little freer tonight with %player_name% on the channel.
Hell yeah! Let's show those Duty stiffs how to actually live out here.
Hey everyone, %player_name% just got their Freedom patch!
Awesome. Hope you like good weed and bad jokes, man.
Don't let the relaxed vibe fool you though. When the bullets fly, we hold our own.
Damn right we do. Welcome to the family.
Another free soul has joined the ranks! Welcome to Freedom, %player_name%.
The Zone is a miracle, not a cage. Glad you see things our way, man.
Just remember that miracle has teeth. Keep your rifle clean for when those brainwashed Monolith fanatics try to push the Barrier.
Exactly. We hold the line so everyone else can breathe. Happy to have you on the wall, brother.
Good news, boys. %player_name% just officially told the military and Duty to shove it.
Haha! Man, I love watching our numbers go up while those stiffs march around in the mud.
No 6 AM reveille, no pointless saluting here. Just good company, great artifacts, and keeping the Zone free.
Just make sure you pull your weight in a firefight. Drop by the base, %player_name%, we'll light something up to celebrate.
%player_name% has taken the oath. Welcome to the line that holds.
Every soldier strengthens the line. %player_name% made the right call.
Deadly anomalies, dangerous mutants, anarchists and bandits... we stop them all.
New recruit: %player_name%. Background checks out. Report for orientation at 0800.
Duty doesn't accept everyone. %player_name% passed screening. That means something.
Keep your weapon maintained and obey orders. You'll do fine.
HQ just confirmed %player_name% is officially Duty personnel.
Good. The Zone is a spreading infection. We need every capable fighter to push it back.
Make sure they memorize the engagement protocols before sending them on patrol.
Already done. For the glorious Duty!
Attention personnel. %player_name% has enlisted in the ranks of Duty.
Good timing. We have a pseudodog pack moving near the Bar. We need guns on the line.
Make sure they know how to handle a rifle first. We don't need liabilities.
If they survive the week, they'll be a proper soldier. Protect the innocent, %player_name%.
I see %player_name% finally realized the Zone isn't a playground.
Exactly. Leave the weed and the campfire songs to those anarchists in Freedom.
Our mission is to protect the outside world. I hope the rookie is ready to bleed for it.
We all bleed. Just make sure you take a dozen mutants down with you. Welcome aboard.
%player_name% threw their lot in with us. Brave, stupid, or both — either works here.
Welcome to the family, %player_name%. Don't touch the stash. Trust takes time.
Or just don't get caught. Hehe.
New blood. %player_name%. Keep them useful and they'll last. Start counting fingers if they don't.
Every new gun is a good gun out here. %player_name% — you're in. Don't make us regret it.
I call dibs on their boots if they step into a burner anomaly.
Word is %player_name% is running with the boys now.
'Bout time. Too much easy money out here to play by the rules.
Stick with us, %player_name%, and you'll be swimming in rubles.
Just remember who gets the boss's cut first. Welcome to the true Zone.
Yo, %player_name% just got patched in. Another brother for the hustle.
Heard they got a quick trigger finger. Perfect for the Garbage checkpoints.
Just tell 'em not to shoot the loners before they hand over the artifacts.
Right on. Dead men don't pay tolls. Welcome to the gang, %player_name%.
Boss says %player_name% is riding with us now. Give 'em some space.
Space? I'll give 'em space when they prove they won't fold under fire.
Don't push it. Let's see how they handle a raid on the stalker camp first.
Fine. Just keep your hands off my cut, %player_name%, and we'll get along fine.
%player_name% is running with us now. Don't get comfortable. Prove yourself first.
New face: %player_name%. I'll believe they're solid when I see it. Out here, talk is cheap.
Just make sure your safety is off when the client says 'go'.
Loyalty out here is earned with blood. Clock starts now, %player_name%.
Keep your weapon clean and follow the contract. That's all that matters.
And never ask what's in the package. Good luck.
Syndicate command updated the roster. %player_name% is officially on the payroll.
Hope they read the fine print. We don't do sick leave.
As long as they hit their targets, I don't care.
Welcome to the private sector, %player_name%. Time is money.
Command confirms %player_name% has signed the syndicate contract. Access codes granted.
Check their loadout. I'm not covering someone using surplus junk.
They meet the minimum gear requirements. Barely.
Then they take point on the next sweep. Let's see what %player_name% is made of.
New contractor on the network. Goes by %player_name%.
Don't care about the name. Do they know the rules of engagement?
No witnesses, no loose ends. Same as always.
Good. Welcome to the real money, %player_name%.
%player_name% has joined Clear Sky's cause. The Zone's patterns will teach them what no textbook can.
%player_name%, welcome. The Zone rewards patience and observation. Impulse gets people killed here.
We observe, we survive, we understand.
New operative: %player_name%. Assign to anomaly mapping — everyone starts there.
Glad to have them. We need every disciplined mind working on understanding what this place is doing.
Grab your Veles detector, %player_name%. There's work to do in the Swamps.
Good to see the roster expanding. %player_name% is with us now.
Let's hope they understand the gravity of our mission. We aren't just here to loot.
If they made it this far into the Swamps, they've got the resolve.
Welcome to Clear Sky, %player_name%. Keep your feet dry.
The Base welcomes %player_name% to our ranks. The Swamps are safer with you.
Don't sugarcoat it. The Swamps are never safe. Watch out for the tall reeds.
True. And keep your eyes peeled for Renegades. They've been pushing our perimeters again.
We study the Zone, but we fight to survive. Welcome to Clear Sky.
I see %player_name% is authorized for field operations. Excellent.
Have they reviewed the latest emission data? We need precise measurements out there.
I'm sending the coordinates now. Just make sure they don't wander into a spatial bubble.
Knowledge is our weapon, %player_name%. Use it well.
Soldier %player_name% has been assigned to Zone containment operations. Report to sector command.
You're military now, %player_name%. That means discipline before everything else. Understood?
Understood. I will send them to the quartermaster for standard issue gear.
New deployment: %player_name%. Follow protocol, keep your head down, do your job.
Copy that. Adding them to the duty roster for perimeter guard.
Keep your eyes open for stalker incursions. Shoot on sight.
Command finally sent us reinforcements. %player_name% just got patched in.
Better late than never. Did they bring extra ammo? We're running dry at the southern checkpoint.
I don't know, ask the quartermaster. Just be glad we have another rifle on the line.
Welcome to the meat grinder, %player_name%. Follow orders and you might go home.
Command post to all units. Private %player_name% is assigned to the southern cordon.
Great, another greenhorn. Tell them to keep their flashlight off at night, or a sniper will do it for them.
And tell them the canned meat is expired. Eat it anyway.
Welcome to the Zone, soldier. Embrace the misery.
Sir, %player_name% has arrived at the checkpoint. Awaiting orders.
Put them on contraband inspection. If stalkers try to cross, confiscate everything.
Does 'confiscate' mean log it in the armory or...?
It means what I said it means, Corporal. Don't ask stupid questions.
Researcher %player_name% has joined the field team. We're glad to have another set of eyes in the Zone.
%player_name%, you've joined the most dangerous laboratory on earth. Try not to become a data point.
Make sure to double-check your SSP-99 seals before leaving the bunker.
Welcome, %player_name%. Brief them on radiation protocols and artifact handling before they go anywhere unsupervised.
Acknowledged. Their field scanner has been synced to our mainframe.
Excellent. Science demands risk, but let's minimize the casualties.
I see %player_name% has signed the expedition contract. Splendid!
Yes, we finally have enough personnel for the Yantar anomaly measurements.
Please inform %player_name% that the emission forecast is stable for the next 48 hours.
Welcome to the pursuit of knowledge, %player_name%. The Zone's secrets await us!
Attention. %player_name% has been granted Level 2 laboratory clearance.
Oh, wonderful! Could they assist with the gravitational anomaly readings in Sector 4?
Only if they've been calibrated for psi-field exposure. We can't afford another... incident.
Safety first, %player_name%. Welcome to the team.
Professor, %player_name% has officially joined the research division.
Excellent timing. We need someone to recover the thermal samples the mercs dropped yesterday.
Are you sure sending a new recruit into a burner field is wise?
Science requires sacrifice, colleague! Just give them plenty of antirad.
